Datapoints: AIPI Index score - 0.35

Stage 1\n Greenfield\n(No Activity): No national AI strategy or policy; absent from government agenda
Stage 2\n Open to Adopt\n(Intent / Plann: Draft strategy or formal consultations underway
Stage 3\nEarly Success\n(Strategy/framew: National AI strategy published
Stage 4\nMaturing\n(Implementation): AI strategy implemented with defined targets and allocated funding
Stage 5\n Role Model\n(Leadership and s: Comprehensive, regularly reviewed AI strategy with measurable outcomes and enforcement mechanisms
Datapoints:
National AI Policy released in 2025 aims to 
- Build a human-centered, ethical, and prosperous Nepal through AI.
- Maximize the use of Artificial Intelligence for the socio-economic development of the nation.
- Build a sustainable and reliable AI ecosystem.
- Accelerate the economic growth rate through the optimal use of AI.
- Enhance the effectiveness of public service delivery through AI applications.
- Strengthen AI governance

Sub-Parameter: AI Governance and Regulatory Frameworks and Ethical AI Principles
Stage 1\n Greenfield\n(No Activity): No governance framework or regulatory discussion; No ethical AI discussion or principles
Stage 2\n Open to Adopt\n(Intent / Plann: Initial discussions on AI governance; no formal framework; Ethical AI principles under discussion or consultation
Stage 3\nEarly Success\n(Strategy/framew: AI governance guidelines or regulatory approach published; Ethical AI principles published
Stage 4\nMaturing\n(Implementation): AI governance framework and ethical AI principles operationalised
Stage 5\n Role Model\n(Leadership and s: AI governance framework and ethical AI principles operationalised; Regulatory body with oversight, enforcement, and review cycles
Datapoints: Improving AI governance mentioned as one of the goals of AI policy but no framework or guidelines mentioned. Same applies for ethical AI principles

Stage 1\n Greenfield\n(No Activity): No government AI initiatives or pilots
Stage 2\n Open to Adopt\n(Intent / Plann: Ad hoc pilots initiated by individual agencies
Stage 3\nEarly Success\n(Strategy/framew: AI projects implemented in some public-sector domains with defined scope
Stage 4\nMaturing\n(Implementation): Multiple AI deployments across ministries and public services; government AI programs with funding and targets
Stage 5\n Role Model\n(Leadership and s: Large-scale AI deployment across government; national AI program with R&D, compute, and talent investment
Datapoints:
1. "Lalitpur Metropolitan City (LMC) has successfully deployed an AI-based intelligent traffic light system in certain major junctions in the city to manage the traffic situation more efficiently. This system uses real-time data to adjust timings at traffic signals automatically.

2. SITA - Developed by UNFPA, in collaboration with the Ministry of Health and Population, UNFPA HQ’s IT Services Office, Google, and with support from the British Embassy Kathmandu.
SITA is an AI platform to  rapidly analyze national datasets and generate detailed insights. It scans thousands of variables in massive datasets like the Nepal Demographic and Health Survey (NDHS), runs the analysis, and outputs polished charts, tables, and even written narratives that match human quality. This helps in making real time decisions at national level."

Stage 1\n Greenfield\n(No Activity): No national DT strategy or roadmap
Stage 2\n Open to Adopt\n(Intent / Plann: Draft DT strategy or consultations underway
Stage 3\nEarly Success\n(Strategy/framew: National DT strategy published with implementation roadmap
Stage 4\nMaturing\n(Implementation): DT strategy implemented with targets, timelines, and dedicated budget
Stage 5\n Role Model\n(Leadership and s: DT strategy mature, regularly updated, and used as a model for regional peers
Datapoints:
Digital Nepal Framework released in 2019 aimed at at leveraging digital technologies to achieve SDGs and harness digital technologies as a catalyst to positively transform government, society and economy. The framework identified 8 sectors and 80 inititaitves to propel socioeconomic growth.

A version 2 of the framework was released in 2025 which has received funding in the form of a $40 million loan co-financed by the ADB and World Bank

e-Governance blueprint, a framework developed by Nepal’s E-governance Board, which comprises representatives from multiple govt. agencies, includes 7 pillars aimed at to addressing existing challenges in digital transformation.

Stage 1\n Greenfield\n(No Activity): No national digital ID system
Stage 2\n Open to Adopt\n(Intent / Plann: Digital ID system under design or piloting
Stage 3\nEarly Success\n(Strategy/framew: Operational national digital ID with basic authentication; enrollment underway
Stage 4\nMaturing\n(Implementation): Digital ID interoperable with at least one other DPI
Stage 5\n Role Model\n(Leadership and s: Fully interoperable digital ID with high population coverage (>70% TAM), privacy safeguards
Datapoints:
Nepal's National Identity Document system or Rastriya Parichaya Patra is a biometric based ID launched in 2018. As of 2025, over 17 million people have been registered which is about 57% of the population and approximately 6 million cards have been printed. 
It is also integrated with Nagarik App (a platform for accessing govt. services) and mandated (in 28 districts) for accessing services like pensions and health insurance. However, owing to the lack of authentication infrastructure agencies still rely on physical documentation and e-KYC is not leveraged

Stage 1\n Greenfield\n(No Activity): No national digital payments infrastructure
Stage 2\n Open to Adopt\n(Intent / Plann: Digital payments system in design or early pilot
Stage 3\nEarly Success\n(Strategy/framew: Operational digital payments system with basic interoperability
Stage 4\nMaturing\n(Implementation): Interoperable digital payments across financial institutions; significant transaction volumes
Stage 5\n Role Model\n(Leadership and s: Universal, fully interoperable digital payments; high financial inclusion; adoption >70% TAM
Datapoints:
Digital Payments system comprises of a National Payment Switch, an interbank payment system and retail payment system developed by Nepal Clearing House developed as a public infrastructure and thereby facilitating interoperability.
It has aslo developed NepalPay QR and NepalPay Instant for real-time payments. Private digital wallets such as eSewa, Khalti and Fonepay also operate in the country.
QR payments are accepted by over 2 million merchants, with volumes growing 117% in a year. In 2024/25, NCHL processed 186 million transactions, covering 17% of total market volume but 77% of value.

Stage 1\n Greenfield\n(No Activity): No data exchange infrastructure or framework
Stage 2\n Open to Adopt\n(Intent / Plann: Data sharing framework under discussion or design
Stage 3\nEarly Success\n(Strategy/framew: Basic data exchange infrastructure operational; limited interoperability between agencies
Stage 4\nMaturing\n(Implementation): Cross-agency data exchange operational with consent mechanisms and defined standards
Stage 5\n Role Model\n(Leadership and s: Mature data exchange ecosystem; extensive partner onboarding-across sectors/institutions
Datapoints: National Data Exchange platform planned which aims to enable enabling multiple government bodies to securely share information through a single interface to reduce duplication and streamline operations

Stage 1\n Greenfield\n(No Activity): No DPI assets operational; no use cases in public or private sector
Stage 2\n Open to Adopt\n(Intent / Plann: Use case of a DPI asset is being planned
Stage 3\nEarly Success\n(Strategy/framew: Use cases of DPI assets across 2–3 sectors in pilot or expansion stage
Stage 4\nMaturing\n(Implementation): Multiple use cases across 4+ sectors, cross-DPI integration in public service delivery
Stage 5\n Role Model\n(Leadership and s: Use case deployment across all government schemes and private sector innovation
Datapoints:
The ‘Nagarik App’ was launched in 2021 to deliver online services through a single online e-governance platform currently offers access to 61 public services. Howerver uptake remains low at around 800,000 users.

SUSASAN has developed digital tools that have been adopted by multiple municipalities in Nepal. These include a data portal, citizen charter, grievance redressal and a municipality mobile app that provides municipality news, notices, services, policies and enables citizens to give feedback to local government
Comments: Digital services reused at municipality level

Stage 1\n Greenfield\n(No Activity): Electricity access <70%;unreliable grid
Stage 2\n Open to Adopt\n(Intent / Plann: Electricity access <70%; national electrification roadmap or grid investment actively underway

Stage 4\nMaturing\n(Implementation): Electricity access >95%; stable grid nationally

Datapoints: 94% of population have access to electricity

Stage 1\n Greenfield\n(No Activity): No cloud or data centre presence; no domestic hosting capacity
Stage 2\n Open to Adopt\n(Intent / Plann: Nascent or imported cloud services only; no domestic compute infrastructure
Stage 3\nEarly Success\n(Strategy/framew: Basic cloud or data hosting via domestic or regional providers
Stage 4\nMaturing\n(Implementation): Compute-capable national data centres operational; domestic and international cloud available
Stage 5\n Role Model\n(Leadership and s: Compute-capable national data centres operational; domestic and international cloud available; Focussed government effort to expand the compute capacity
Datapoints:
Domestic IT and global firms providing cloud hosting services

Governemnt in partnership with these cloud providers has developed its own cloud infrastructure in the form of  Government Integrated Data Center (GIDC)

Stage 1\n Greenfield\n(No Activity): Large access gaps across geographic, gender, and socioeconomic dimensions; no disaggregated data collected
Stage 2\n Open to Adopt\n(Intent / Plann: Inclusion gaps identified and measured; pilot programmes or targeted interventions initiated
Stage 3\nEarly Success\n(Strategy/framew: Inclusion programmes operational;gaps narrowing with measurable progress
Stage 4\nMaturing\n(Implementation): Gaps significantly narrowed- rural-urban gap <5 percentage points, gender gap <5 percentage points; affrodable infrastructure available nationally
Stage 5\n Role Model\n(Leadership and s: Near equitable access across all dimensions
Datapoints: Internet access stands at 65% in urban vs 22% in rural areas

Sub-Parameter:
WB Worldwide Governance Indicators - Political Stability Score
2024
Stage 1\n Greenfield\n(No Activity): 0 – 20th percentile
Stage 2\n Open to Adopt\n(Intent / Plann: 20 – 40th percentile
Stage 3\nEarly Success\n(Strategy/framew: 40 – 60th percentile
Stage 4\nMaturing\n(Implementation): 60 – 80th percentile
Stage 5\n Role Model\n(Leadership and s: 80 – 100th percentile
Datapoints:
Worldwide governance index score - 39.34. Scores of the sub parameters are as follows

-Voice and Accountability (The extent to which citizens can participate in the selection of government, their freedom of expression and holding officials accountable) : 55.80
-Political Stability (Measures political motivated violance and/or terrorism) : 58.81
-Government Effectiveness (Quality of public services) : 34.14
-Regulatory Quality (Ability to implement sound policy and laws) : 46.15
- Rule of Law (Quality of law enforcement) : 51.74 (Ranked 72 out of 143, as of 2025)
-Control of Corruption (Captures perceptions of extent to which public power could be misused for private gain) : 35.05 (Ranked 107 out of 143, as of 2025)

Characterised by high instability with the country undergoing  14 government changes since 2008

Sub-Parameter: Political stability and DT policy continuity (Requirement of KIIs)
Stage 1\n Greenfield\n(No Activity): High instability or conflict; no digital transformation agenda
Stage 2\n Open to Adopt\n(Intent / Plann: Fragile stability; digital agenda exists but is ad hoc and leadership-dependent
Stage 3\nEarly Success\n(Strategy/framew: Stable government; digital transformation is a formal priority and survives a change of administration
Stage 4\nMaturing\n(Implementation): Sustained stability; digital transformation embedded in national agenda across election cycles
Stage 5\n Role Model\n(Leadership and s: Full stability, digital transformation is a cross-party strategic priority
Datapoints: High political instability but digital transformation efforts have sustained

Stage 3\nEarly Success\n(Strategy/framew: Inter-ministerial coordination mechanism established
Stage 4\nMaturing\n(Implementation): Inter-ministerial coordination mechanism established; lead agency with clear mandate

Datapoints: Ministry of Communication and Information Technology and the Nepal e-governance board (part of office of Prime Minsiter and Council of Ministers)

Stage 1\n Greenfield\n(No Activity): No private sector engagement in digital agenda
Stage 2\n Open to Adopt\n(Intent / Plann: Ad hoc engagement with select tech or telecom operators
Stage 3\nEarly Success\n(Strategy/framew: Participation or consultation with private sector; PPP frameworks emerging
Stage 4\nMaturing\n(Implementation): Formal public-private platforms operational; DPG and open-source ecosystem engaged; fintech and cloud providers integrated in delivery
Stage 5\n Role Model\n(Leadership and s: Deeper public-private integration; private sector co-invests in national digital infrastructure
Datapoints:
Private sector firms have engaged 
- In Development of  Government Integrated Data Center 
- Interoperable digital payment networks - Eg. Fonepay Payment service Ltd is licensed as a payment system operator by Nepal Rastra Bank, the country's central bank. It also collaborated with NPCI to facilitate UPI payments in Nepal
- DPG development - Rumsan Associates has developed Rahat, a collection of open-source blockchain-based digital relief distribution management projects. This  can be used by humanitarian agencies to support marginalized communities

Stage 1\n Greenfield\n(No Activity): No MDB or bilateral engagement
Stage 2\n Open to Adopt\n(Intent / Plann: MDB/donor activity exploratory; primarily providing advisory or strategy support
Stage 3\nEarly Success\n(Strategy/framew: Recurrent MDB and bilateral investment in AI/DPI projects; approved and pipeline financing visible
Stage 4\nMaturing\n(Implementation): Multi-year strategic partnerships with ADB, World Bank, bilaterals operational
Stage 5\n Role Model\n(Leadership and s: Development partners fully aligned to national roadmap
Datapoints:
UNDP Nepal implemented development programmes across Nepal in 2021 with a total expenditure of $20.4 million.

The World Bank and the ADB jointly co-finance the Nepal Digital Transformation Project contributing $50 million and $ 40 million respectively. It aims to invest in digital platforms including an integrated online citizen service portal and an integrated social registry; a secure government-wide data exchange; and a digital locker to manage verifiable credentials and digital wallet.

However, in 2024, the World Bank cancelled a $110 million loan agreement for the Digital Nepal Acceleration project, two years after signingdue to the lack of clarity on implementation

It ensures  the right to privacy of every individual over their data in the online medium and asserts restrictions on processing and infringment of privacy

Stage 1\n Greenfield\n(No Activity): No national cybersecurity strategy, legislation, or institutional framework
Stage 2\n Open to Adopt\n(Intent / Plann: Cybersecurity strategy or legislation under discussion
Stage 3\nEarly Success\n(Strategy/framew: National cybersecurity strategy and/or law published
Stage 4\nMaturing\n(Implementation): Cybersecurity framework operational with enforcement mechanisms; designated agency for cybersecurity established
Stage 5\n Role Model\n(Leadership and s: Cybersecurity frameworks/law enforced; designated authority for cybersecurity; regular incident reporting and response systems active
Datapoints:
The National CyberSecurity Policy released in 2023 aims to 
-Establish legal and institutional frameworks to build a secure cyber space.
-Protect critical national infrastructure while reducing the risk of cyberattacks.
-Enhance cyber security research, human resource development and enhancing capacity of existing workforce, to ensure a robust and strong cyber space.
-Improve the reliability and security of digital technology-based services.
-Exchange bilateral, regional and international coordination, knowledge sharing, and assistance to reduce cyber security related risks.
- Increase Nepal's Global Cyber Security Index (GCI) score from the current 44.99% to 60% within the next 5 years,

Sub-Parameter: AIPI is the sum of four key dimensions: digital infrastructure, human capital, technological innovation, and legal frameworks. These four dimensions are likely relevant for smooth AI adoption. In turn, each dimension is computed by normalizing and averaging a rich set of sub-indicators including, but not limited to, the presence of relevant digital infrastructure, sustained human capital investment, inclusive STEM expertise, labor and capital mobility, a vibrant R&D ecosystem, and the adaptability of legal frameworks to digital business models.

Parameter:
World Bank  - Worldwide Governance Indicators (1996-2024)

For this study, 2024 data has been considered
Sub-Parameter:
The WGI draw on 35 cross-country data sources—including household surveys, firm surveys, and expert assessments—and are annual composite indicators based on perceptions of governance quality across more than 200 economies from 1996 to 2024.

Across these 35 data sources, more than 400 underlying indicators are used to capture the six dimensions of governance included in the WGI:

Voice and Accountability
Political Stability
Government Effectiveness
Regulatory Quality
Rule of Law
Control of Corruption

Political stability- perceptions of the extent to which political power and governance are secure from destabilization, and of the likelihood that authority will be challenged or altered through violent, coercive, or unconstitutional means.
